```
│       │                  │  gRPC   │       │                    │
│  [Sensor Drivers]        │  5G/WiFi│  [Fleet Manager]           │
│  [Actuator Drivers]      │         │       │                    │
└──────────────────────────┘         └────────────────────────────┘
```

The boundary between layers is governed by:

$$
t_{\text{task}} \; \overset{?}{\gtreqless} \; t_{\text{loop,real-time}}
$$

**Part (b) — Real-Time Feasibility Calculation**

Given:

- On-board motion control loop: $f_{\text{loop}} = 1 \text{ kHz}$ → loop period $T_{\text{loop}} = 1 \text{ ms}$.
- Cloud perception + grasp pipeline latency: $t_{\text{cloud}} = 80 \text{ ms}$.
- One-way network latency assumption: $t_{\text{net}} \approx 10 \text{ ms}$ (typical 5G).

Total worst-case latency from sensor capture to grasp command available on the robot:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{total}} &= t_{\text{net}} + t_{\text{cloud}} + t_{\text{net,return}} \\[4pt]
                 &= 10 \text{ ms} + 80 \text{ ms} + 10 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
                 &= 100 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compare with the motion loop period:

$$
t_{\text{total}} = 100 \text{ ms} \;\gg\; T_{\text{loop}} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

**Decision:**

The grasping decision **cannot** be used inside the real-time motion loop (which requires deterministic 1 ms execution). However, the cloud grasp planner is acceptable for **supervisory-level** grasping decisions, where the robot pauses briefly, requests a grasp from the cloud, and then executes it locally.

**Design Implication:** A hybrid architecture is required:

- **Real-time tracking** → on-board.
- **High-level grasp selection** → cloud offload (tolerates 100 ms latency).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 2 — Robotics Design):**
> 1. Confusing **DOF** with *number of joints* — they are not the same; joints contribute differently (revolute = 1, prismatic = 1, spherical = 3).
> 2. Writing DH parameter table without mentioning the **sign convention** for $x_i$ along the common normal — examiners deduct up to 1 mark.
> 3. In cloud-robotics questions, students often say "offload everything" — **WRONG**. Safety, motion control, and emergency stop **must stay on-board**. Always justify the partitioning.
> 4. Forgetting to convert kHz to seconds when comparing loop period vs. cloud latency (1 mark deduction).
> 5. Skipping the **nonlinearity comment** in forward kinematics — examiners expect at least one sentence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **DOF Definition:** Minimum coordinates to fully specify robot configuration.
- **Grübler’s Formula:** $M = \lambda (n - j - 1) + \sum f_i$ — apply with spatial $\lambda = 6$ for 3D arms.
- **DH Convention:** Four parameters per link: $\theta, d, a, \alpha$. Place $z_i$ along joint axis, $x_i$ along common normal.
- **Forward Kinematics:** Multiplication of homogeneous transforms; nonlinear in joint angles.
- **Inverse Kinematics:** Closed-form only for special structures; otherwise numerical.
- **Workspace:** Reachable $\supseteq$ Dexterous.
- **Repeatability $\neq$ Accuracy.**
- **Cloud Offloading Rule:** Real-time loops (1 ms–10 ms) **never** go to cloud. Soft real-time (50 ms+) is acceptable.
- **Standard Flange:** ISO 9409-1 for industrial arms.
- **Standard Frame:** ROS 2 + URDF for interoperability in cloud robotics.
- **Network Targets:** < 50 ms teleop; < 500 ms supervisory.
- **Always-on Safety:** PLC + hardware E-stop independent of cloud connectivity.
- **Kinematic chain products:** Use shorthand $c_{ijk} = \cos(\theta_i+\theta_j+\theta_k)$.
- **Mobility check is a sanity test**, not a substitute for full kinematic synthesis.
- **Design is iterative** — never freeze specs after a single simulation pass.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT866 - CLOUD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Navigation Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Navigation Planning** in Cloud Robotics is defined as the autonomous computational process of determining a geometrically feasible, collision-free, and kinodynamically optimal trajectory for a robotic agent from an initial configuration to a goal configuration, executed by leveraging distributed cloud resources (compute, storage, map databases) to overcome the onboard limitations of memory, processing power, and real-time responsiveness.

In the context of **KTU PERUT866 (Cloud Robotics)**, navigation planning is treated as a **hierarchical decision-making problem** comprising three tightly coupled sub-layers:
1. **Global Path Planning** — coarse, map-level route generation.
2. **Local Motion Planning** — fine-grained, reactive trajectory execution.
3. **Replanning / Recovery** — dynamic adaptation when the world model changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The distinguishing feature of cloud-augmented navigation is the *offloading* of computationally expensive search (e.g., A*, RRT*, Dynamic Programming) to the cloud, while the edge/robot handles low-latency reactive control. This is the **"Thin-Client, Fat-Cloud"** paradigm.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The GPS + Driver Duo:**
> Imagine you are travelling from **Kochi to Delhi** in a car. Your **Google Maps** (running on a powerful cloud server) calculates the *best highway route* in 2 seconds using a giant road database. This is **Global Planning**.
>
> Now, mid-journey, a cow suddenly walks onto the road. You, the **driver**, must instantly swerve or brake. You don't call the cloud for this — you react in milliseconds. This is **Local / Reactive Planning**.
>
> After avoiding the cow, you call Maps again and say, *"Re-route me from my new location."* This is **Cloud Replanning**.
>
> In a **cloud robot**, the robot = car, the cloud = Google Maps, and the onboard controller = the driver. Navigation Planning is the **continuous dialogue between driver and maps**.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Engineering Constants)

The following quantitative benchmarks are used industry-wide to evaluate any cloud-based navigation planner:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Target |
|---|---|---|
| Path Optimality Ratio | $\rho$ | $\rho \le 1.25$ of optimal |
| Planning Latency (Cloud RTT) | $t_{cloud}$ | $\le \mathbf{100\;ms}$ |
| Control Loop Frequency | $f_c$ | $\ge \mathbf{50\;Hz}$ |
| Position Error (SLAM) | $\epsilon_p$ | $\le \mathbf{0.05\;m}$ |
| Map Coverage | $C_{map}$ | $\ge \mathbf{95\%}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Optimality Ratio $\rho$:** The ratio of the length of the path generated by the planner to the length of the mathematically optimal (shortest) path. A value of 1.0 means perfect optimality.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Navigation Planning in Cloud Robotics

1. **Perception (SLAM / Mapping Layer):** Builds and updates the occupancy grid or topological map — usually stored in the cloud as a shared World Model.
2. **Decision (Planning Layer):** Generates the trajectory — the *brain* of the system, executed on cloud VMs or serverless functions.
3. **Action (Control Layer):** Translates the trajectory into wheel/motor commands — strictly onboard for real-time safety.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Navigation Planning Problem

The complete navigation problem $\mathcal{N}$ can be formally expressed as a tuple:

$$
\mathcal{N} = (C_{free},\, q_{start},\, q_{goal},\, U,\, t)
$$

Where:
- $C_{free} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ — set of collision-free configurations in the configuration space (C-space).
- $q_{start} \in C_{free}$ — initial robot configuration.
- $q_{goal} \in C_{free}$ — desired goal configuration.
- $U$ — set of admissible control inputs / motion primitives.
- $t$ — time horizon (for dynamic problems).

The planner seeks a continuous function:

$$
\pi: [0,\, T] \rightarrow C_{free} \quad \text{with} \quad \pi(0)=q_{start}, \; \pi(T)=q_{goal}
$$

### 2.2 Why Cloud Offloading? — The Latency-Bandwidth Tradeoff

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rationale:** Onboard processors (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi) have **strict thermal, power, and weight constraints**. Algorithms like A* on a $1000\times1000$ grid with $10^6$ nodes can take **seconds** onboard, but **milliseconds** on a cloud GPU. The cloud is essential for *global* search; onboard is essential for *local* safety.

### 2.3 Classification of Path Planning Algorithms (KTU-Mandated Taxonomy)

**A. Graph / Search-Based (Deterministic, Complete)**
- **Dijkstra's Algorithm** — uninformed, finds shortest path, $O(V^2)$.
- **A\* Algorithm** — heuristic-guided, $O(b^d)$, optimal if $h(n)$ is admissible.
- **D\* / D\* Lite** — dynamic replanning for unknown/moving obstacles.
- **ARA\*** — Anytime Repairing A*, returns best-so-far solution within time budget.

**B. Sampling-Based (Probabilistic, Resolution-Complete)**
- **PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap)** — multi-query, builds a roadmap graph.
- **RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree)** — single-query, kinematically feasible.
- **RRT\*** — asymptotically optimal variant of RRT.
- **Informed RRT\*** — uses heuristic to focus sampling inside an ellipsoidal heuristic region.

**C. Reactive / Behavior-Based (Local, Real-Time)**
- **Potential Fields** — attractive goal + repulsive obstacles.
- **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** — samples in velocity space.
- **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** — polar histogram obstacle representation.

**D. Optimization-Based (Modern Cloud Approach)**
- **MPC (Model Predictive Control)** — solves an optimization at each step.
- **Trajectory Optimization** — CHOMP, STOMP, with cloud-parallel rollouts.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these 10 equations. They cover $> 90\%$ of KTU numerical and theory questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning of Each Term | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A* Evaluation | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $g(n)$ = cost-so-far; $h(n)$ = estimated cost-to-go; $f(n)$ = total estimated cost | dimensionless / meters |
| 2 | Manhattan Distance | $h_{M}(n) = \vert x_{goal} - x_n \vert + \vert y_{goal} - y_n \vert$ | 4-connected grid heuristic | meters |
| 3 | Euclidean Distance | $h_{E}(n) = \sqrt{(x_{goal}-x_n)^2 + (y_{goal}-y_n)^2}$ | 8-connected grid heuristic | meters |
| 4 | Diagonal (Octile) | $h_{O}(n) = D \cdot \max(\Delta x,\Delta y) + (D_2 - D) \cdot \min(\Delta x,\Delta y)$ | 8-connected with $D=1, D_2=\sqrt{2}$ | meters |
| 5 | Dijkstra Cost Update | $d[v] = \min(d[v],\, d[u] + w(u,v))$ | Standard relaxation of edge $(u,v)$ | meters |
| 6 | Potential Field Force | $\mathbf{F}_{total} = \mathbf{F}_{att} + \mathbf{F}_{rep} = -k_a \nabla U_{att} - k_r \nabla U_{rep}$ | Sum of attractive and repulsive forces | Newtons |
| 7 | Attractive Potential | $U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, k_a\, \vert\vert q - q_{goal} \vert\vert^2$ | Quadratic near goal | dimensionless |
| 8 | Repulsive Potential | $U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\, k_r \left(\tfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2, & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$ | Active within distance $\rho_0$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | RRT Vertices (Asymptotic) | $\lim_{n \to \infty} V_n = V_{connected}$ | Probability of finding solution → 1 as samples $\to \infty$ | — |
| 10 | Cloud RTT (Worst Case) | $t_{total} = t_{sense} + t_{uplink} + t_{plan} + t_{downlink} + t_{actuate}$ | End-to-end navigation loop | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Admissible Heuristic:** A heuristic $h(n)$ is *admissible* if it never overestimates the true cost to the goal: $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$. This guarantees A\* returns an optimal path.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva):** Cloud-based global A* on a shared map; onboard DWA for local avoidance.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo):** Hybrid A* + RRT* for parking; MPC for longitudinal control.
- **Drone Swarms (DJI Agras):** Cloud mission planning; onboard obstacle avoidance.
- **Service Robots (Pepper, Spot):** Topological navigation in indoor maps stored in the cloud.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — A* Heuristic Admissibility Proof (Sketch)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** A* optimality is a frequent 14-mark question. The proof uses the *consistency* of the heuristic.

**Claim:** If $h(n)$ is admissible, then A* returns an optimal path.

**Step 1 — Define Optimal Path Cost:**
Let $C^*$ be the cost of the optimal path from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$.

**Step 2 — Consider the First Node $n^*$ on the Optimal Path Expanded by A*:**
A* expands nodes in order of increasing $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.

**Step 3 — At the moment of expanding $n^*$:**
- $g(n^*) = g^*(n^*)$ (the cost-so-far along the optimal prefix).
- $h(n^*) \le h^*(n^*)$ (admissibility).

Therefore:

$$
f(n^*) = g(n^*) + h(n^*) \le g^*(n^*) + h^*(n^*) = C^*
$$

**Step 4 — Any Sub-Optimal Node $n'$ Has:**
For any node $n'$ on a sub-optimal path with $g(n') + h(n') > C^*$:

$$
f(n') > C^* \ge f(n^*)
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
A* will always expand $n^*$ before any sub-optimal node $n'$ with $f(n') > f(n^*)$. Therefore, when $q_{goal}$ is expanded, the path is optimal. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** Admissibility = "Never lie by exaggerating the remaining distance." Euclidean distance on a flat map is admissible because the straight line is the shortest possible path.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Potential Field Local Minima Escape

**The Problem:** A robot at $q$ may get stuck in a local minimum where $\mathbf{F}_{total} = 0$ but $q \ne q_{goal}$.

**Step 1 — Force Definitions:**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{att}(q) = -k_a (q - q_{goal})
$$

$$
\mathbf{F}_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} k_r \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \tfrac{1}{\rho^2(q)} \, \nabla \rho(q), & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Local Minimum Condition:**
At a local minimum $q_{min}$:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{total}(q_{min}) = \mathbf{F}_{att}(q_{min}) + \sum_{i} \mathbf{F}_{rep,i}(q_{min}) = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 3 — Random Walk Escape (Cloud-Side Recovery):**
A cloud-augmented planner injects a random perturbation:

$$
q_{next} = q_{min} + \eta \cdot \mathbf{v}_{random}, \quad \mathbf{v}_{random} \in \mathcal{S}^{n-1}
$$

where $\eta$ is the escape step size (typically $0.1$ m to $0.5$ m) and $\mathcal{S}^{n-1}$ is the unit sphere of random directions.

**Step 4 — Re-evaluation in Cloud:**
The cloud re-evaluates $\mathbf{F}_{total}(q_{next})$ and proceeds if the new position breaks the minimum.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Mention **"Random Restarts"** or **"Walls-following heuristic"** as escape strategies — these are favorite board-exam keywords.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Cloud Offloading Time Budget

**Scenario:** A robot must replan in 200 ms. Onboard A* takes 800 ms. Cloud A* takes 60 ms compute + 40 ms uplink + 40 ms downlink = 140 ms total.

**Step 1 — Compute Feasibility:**

$$
t_{total} = t_{uplink} + t_{plan} + t_{downlink} = 40 + 60 + 40 = 140 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
t_{total} \le t_{deadline} = 200 \text{ ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Offloading is feasible.}
$$

**Step 2 — Speedup Factor:**

$$
S = \frac{t_{onboard}}{t_{cloud}} = \frac{800}{140} \approx 5.71
$$

**Step 3 — Energy Efficiency (Edge Battery Savings):**

$$
E_{saved} = P_{onboard} \cdot t_{onboard} - P_{comm} \cdot t_{comm}
$$

Where $P_{onboard} = 15$ W (full CPU load) and $P_{comm} = 2$ W (radio).

$$
E_{saved} = 15 \times 0.8 - 2 \times 0.14 = 12.0 - 0.28 = 11.72 \text{ J per replan}
$$

For 100 replans/hour:

$$
E_{saved,\;hourly} = 1172 \text{ J} = 0.325 \text{ Wh}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** For mobile robots with limited battery, offloading 1.17 kJ/hour of compute to the cloud directly extends mission duration by several minutes per day.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection

| Algorithm | Completeness | Optimality | Time Complexity | Best Use Case in Cloud Robotics |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dijkstra | Complete | Optimal | $O(V^2)$ or $O(E + V\log V)$ with heap | Small static maps onboard |
| A* | Complete | Optimal (admissible $h$) | $O(b^d)$ exponential in worst case | **Cloud global planning** (large grids) |
| D* Lite | Complete | Optimal | $O(E + V\log V)$ incremental | **Cloud replanning** in dynamic maps |
| RRT | Probabilistically complete | Sub-optimal | $O(n \log n)$ | High-dimensional C-space (arms) |
| RRT* | Asymptotically optimal | Optimal as $n \to \infty$ | $O(n \log n)$ | Cloud-parallel motion planning |
| PRM | Probabilistically complete | Sub-optimal | $O(n \log n)$ | Multi-query static environments |
| Potential Field | Not complete (local min) | Local optimum | $O(1)$ per step | **Onboard reactive avoidance** |
| DWA | Local complete | Local optimum | $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert \omega \vert)$ | **Onboard velocity control** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cloud-Augmented Navigation Planning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sensorLayer["Onboard Sensors\nLIDAR Camera IMU"]
    edgeNode["Edge Controller\nJetson Nano 50Hz Loop"]
    cloudPlanner["Cloud Planner\nAstar RRT MPC"]
    worldModel["Shared World Model\nMap Database"]
    actuatorLayer["Motor Drivers\nWheel Commands"]
    goalInput["Mission Goal\nFrom Operator"]
    
    sensorLayer --> edgeNode
    edgeNode -->|"Telemetry Uplink\n50Hz Compressed"| cloudPlanner
    cloudPlanner --> worldModel
    goalInput --> cloudPlanner
    cloudPlanner -->|"Global Path\nWaypoints"| edgeNode
    edgeNode -->|"DWA Local\nReactive Cmd"| actuatorLayer
    actuatorLayer -.->|"Motion Feedback"| edgeNode
    worldModel -.->|"Map Updates\n5Hz Push"| cloudPlanner
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The cloud handles *global* search using A* or RRT*, the edge handles *local* reactive control with DWA, and the world model is a shared resource. This is the canonical **Cloud Robotics Navigation Stack**.

---

### 4.2 A* Search Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    init["Initialize OpenSet\nwith StartNode"]
    closedSet["ClosedSet Empty"]
    popBest["Pop Node n\nwith minimum f(n)"]
    checkGoal{"n equals Goal?"}
    returnPath["Return Reconstructed Path"]
    expandN["Expand Neighbors\nof n"]
    computeF["f n = g n + h n"]
    checkInOpen{"Neighbor in\nOpenSet?"}
    updateBetter{"g via n less\nthan existing?"}
    updateNode["Update g n and parent"]
    addOpen["Add to OpenSet"]
    failCheck{"OpenSet\nEmpty?"}
    noPath["Return No Path"]
    addClosed["Add n to ClosedSet"]
    
    init --> closedSet
    init --> popBest
    popBest --> failCheck
    failCheck -->|"Yes"| noPath
    failCheck -->|"No"| checkGoal
    checkGoal -->|"Yes"| returnPath
    checkGoal -->|"No"| addClosed
    addClosed --> expandN
    expandN --> computeF
    computeF --> checkInOpen
    checkInOpen -->|"No"| addOpen
    checkInOpen -->|"Yes"| updateBetter
    updateBetter -->|"Yes"| updateNode
    updateBetter -->|"No"| popBest
    addOpen --> popBest
    updateNode --> popBest
```

---

### 4.3 Path Planning Algorithm Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["Navigation Planning"]
    
    global["Global Planning\nCloud Side"]
    local["Local Planning\nEdge Side"]
    replan["Replanning Layer\nCloud Triggered"]
    
    aStar["Astar Algorithm\nDijkstra"]
    rrt["RRT RRTstar\nPRM"]
    
    dwa["Dynamic Window\nApproach"]
    potField["Potential Field\nVFH"]
    
    dStar["D Star Lite\nARA Star"]
    informed["Informed RRTstar\nAnytime Planner"]
    
    root --> global
    root --> local
    root --> replan
    
    global --> aStar
    global --> rrt
    
    local --> dwa
    local --> potField
    
    replan --> dStar
    replan --> informed
```

---

### 4.4 SLAM + Navigation Coupling in Cloud

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawData["Raw Sensor Data\nPoint Clouds"]
    localSlam["Onboard SLAM\nOdometry Pose Graph"]
    cloudSlam["Cloud SLAM\nLoop Closure\nMap Merging"]
    sharedMap["Shared Occupancy Grid\nStored in Cloud DB"]
    planner["Global Path Planner\nAstar RRTstar"]
    controller["Local Controller\nDWA"]
    robot["Robot Actuators"]
    
    rawData --> localSlam
    localSlam -->|"Pose Updates\nCompressed 5Hz"| cloudSlam
    cloudSlam --> sharedMap
    sharedMap --> planner
    planner -->|"Waypoints 1Hz"| controller
    controller --> robot
    robot -.->|"Feedback"| localSlam
    cloudSlam -.->|"Loop Closure\nCorrections"| localSlam
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the **separation between cloud-side (slow but powerful) and edge-side (fast but limited)**. Examiners specifically look for this cloud-edge delineation in architecture diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Navigation Planning. Differentiate between Global Path Planning and Local Motion Planning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Navigation Planning is the process of determining a collision-free, feasible trajectory for a robot from a start configuration to a goal configuration, often by leveraging cloud resources for compute-intensive search.

| Aspect | Global Path Planning | Local Motion Planning |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Entire map from start to goal | Immediate vicinity of robot |
| Map Used | Complete, pre-built map | Local, sensor-derived window |
| Where Computed | Cloud (offloaded) | Onboard (real-time) |
| Latency Tolerance | High (seconds) | Very low (10–50 ms) |
| Algorithm Examples | A*, RRT*, Dijkstra | DWA, Potential Field, VFH |
| Optimality | Globally optimal | Locally optimal only |
| Obstacle Knowledge | Static, known a priori | Dynamic, sensor-detected |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the comparison table.)*

---

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is an admissible heuristic? Why is Euclidean distance admissible for A\* on a 2D grid?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A heuristic function $h(n)$ is called **admissible** if it *never overestimates* the true minimum cost $h^*(n)$ from node $n$ to the goal; i.e., $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all nodes $n$.

Euclidean distance is admissible for 2D grid planning because the straight-line distance between two points is the *shortest possible path length* in continuous 2D space. Since any actual path (which must follow grid edges, navigate around obstacles, or curve) can never be shorter than the straight line, Euclidean distance can never overestimate — and therefore it is admissible.

A* using an admissible heuristic is guaranteed to return an *optimal path* (a fundamental theorem of A*).

*(Award 1 mark for admissibility definition, 1 mark for Euclidean reasoning, 1 mark for optimality guarantee.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **cloud-based hierarchical navigation architecture** for a mobile robot. Clearly delineate the responsibilities of the **cloud layer, edge layer, and sensor/actuator layer**. Discuss why such a hierarchical split is **necessary** rather than running everything onboard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **A\* algorithm** for global path planning. Derive the optimality condition using the admissibility of the heuristic $h(n)$. Apply A* step-by-step on a $4\times4$ grid (with one blocked cell) to find the shortest path from $(0,0)$ to $(3,3)$ using **Manhattan distance** as the heuristic. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cloud-Based Hierarchical Navigation Architecture — 7 marks**

Refer to **Section 4.1** diagram. The architecture has three layers:

1. **Sensor/Actuator Layer (Hardware):** LIDAR, camera, IMU, wheel encoders, motor drivers. This is the *physical interface* with the world.

2. **Edge Layer (Onboard Compute):** Runs on Jetson/RPi. Performs *local SLAM*, *reactive obstacle avoidance* (DWA, Potential Field), and *low-level control loops* at $\ge 50$ Hz. Critical for safety — must respond within milliseconds.

3. **Cloud Layer (Remote Compute):** Hosts the *global planner* (A*, RRT*), *shared world model*, *multi-robot coordination*, and *map database*. Communicates with edge via Wi-Fi/5G.

**Why Hierarchical Split is Necessary (3 marks justification):**

- **Onboard limitations:** Limited CPU (typically 4–8 cores, 1–2 GHz), limited RAM (4–16 GB), tight thermal envelope (5–15 W TDP).
- **Algorithmic complexity:** A* on a $1000\times1000$ grid has $10^6$ states; RRT* for a 7-DOF arm has a vast C-space. Onboard computation can take **seconds to minutes**.
- **Cloud advantages:** Elastic GPUs, parallel rollout execution, shared map across robot fleet, persistent storage.
- **Safety constraint:** Local reactive loops *cannot tolerate* cloud latency; they must run onboard. But the cloud can pre-compute the global route.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often state *"we offload everything to the cloud."* This loses 2 marks. The correct answer is **selective offloading** — global planning to cloud, local safety to edge. Mention **latency-bound partitioning** explicitly.

---

**(b) A\* Algorithm — 7 marks**

**Algorithm (2 marks):**
- Maintain an **Open Set** (priority queue of nodes to explore, ordered by $f(n)$).
- Maintain a **Closed Set** (already-explored nodes).
- At each step, pop the node with minimum $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.
- Expand neighbors; for each, compute tentative $g$ via current node. If better, update.
- Stop when goal is popped, or Open Set is empty (failure).

**Optimality Derivation (2 marks):**
As shown in **Section 3.1**, if $h$ is admissible, A* expands all optimal-path nodes before any sub-optimal node, because $f(n^*) \le C^* \le f(n')$ for any sub-optimal $n'$. Therefore the returned path is optimal.

**Worked Example (3 marks):** $4 \times 4$ grid, start $S=(0,0)$, goal $G=(3,3)$, blocked cell at $(2,2)$.

Step-by-step trace table (Manhattan heuristic, $h(n) = \vert x-3 \vert + \vert y-3 \vert$):

| Step | Pop | $g$ | $h$ | $f$ | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(0,0)$ | 0 | 6 | 6 | Add $(1,0),(0,1)$ |
| 2 | $(1,0)$ | 1 | 5 | 6 | Add $(2,0),(1,1)$ |
| 3 | $(0,1)$ | 1 | 5 | 6 | Add $(1,1),(0,2)$ (skip $(0,0)$) |
| 4 | $(2,0)$ | 2 | 4 | 6 | Add $(3,0),(2,1)$ |
| 5 | $(1,1)$ | 2 | 4 | 6 | Add $(2,1),(1,2),(0,1)$ — update where better |
| 6 | $(3,0)$ | 3 | 3 | 6 | Add $(3,1)$ |
| 7 | $(0,2)$ | 2 | 4 | 6 | Add $(1,2),(0,3)$ |
| 8 | $(2,1)$ | 3 | 3 | 6 | Add $(3,1),(2,2)$ — $(2,2)$ blocked, skip |
| 9 | $(1,2)$ | 3 | 3 | 6 | Add $(2,2)$ blocked, $(1,3)$ |
| 10 | $(3,1)$ | 4 | 2 | 6 | Add $(3,2)$ |
| 11 | $(0,3)$ | 3 | 3 | 6 | Add $(1,3)$ |
| 12 | $(3,2)$ | 5 | 1 | 6 | Add $(3,3)$ |
| 13 | $(1,3)$ | 4 | 2 | 6 | Already optimal |
| 14 | $(3,3)$ | 6 | 0 | 6 | **GOAL REACHED** |

**Optimal Path:** $(0,0) \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,0) \to (3,1) \to (3,2) \to (3,3)$ with cost $g = 6$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Common Errors:**
> 1. Using Euclidean instead of Manhattan in a 4-connected grid — loses 1 mark.
> 2. Forgetting to check if a neighbor is in the *Closed* set (re-expansion waste).
> 3. Reporting the path as the **first goal encountered** rather than the one with minimum $f$ at pop time.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT)** algorithm. State its key properties (completeness, optimality). How does **RRT\*** improve on RRT, and what is the meaning of **asymptotic optimality**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot must navigate in a **dynamic environment** where obstacles can appear and disappear. Explain how the **Potential Field method** works for local reactive planning. Discuss the **local minima problem** and propose **two cloud-assisted escape strategies**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RRT and RRT\* — 7 marks**

**RRT Algorithm (3 marks):**

1. Start with tree $\mathcal{T}$ containing only $q_{start}$.
2. Repeat until goal reached or iteration limit:
   a. Sample a random configuration $q_{rand} \in C_{free}$.
   b. Find the nearest node $q_{near} \in \mathcal{T}$ to $q_{rand}$ (Euclidean distance).
   c. Extend from $q_{near}$ toward $q_{rand}$ by a fixed step $\epsilon$, producing $q_{new}$.
   d. If the edge $(q_{near}, q_{new})$ is collision-free, add $q_{new}$ to $\mathcal{T}$ with parent $q_{near}$.
3. Return path by tracing parents from $q_{goal}$ to $q_{start}$.

**Key Properties of RRT (2 marks):**

- **Probabilistically Complete:** Probability of finding a solution (if one exists) approaches 1 as the number of samples $n \to \infty$.
- **Not Optimal:** The first path found is usually long and jagged; RRT makes no attempt to minimize cost.

**RRT\* Improvements (1 mark):**

- After adding $q_{new}$, RRT* **rewires** the tree: for each neighbor $q_{nb} \in \mathcal{T}$ within radius $r$, check if going through $q_{new}$ yields a shorter path; if so, re-parent $q_{nb}$.

- **Asymptotic Optimality:** As $n \to \infty$, the cost of the best path found by RRT* converges to the optimal cost $C^*$. Formally:

$$
P\!\left(\limsup_{n \to \infty} C_n^{best} = C^*\right) = 1
$$

**(b) Potential Field Method in Dynamic Environments — 7 marks**

**Method (3 marks):** The robot is treated as a point moving under a virtual force field:
- $\mathbf{F}_{att} = -k_a (q - q_{goal})$ — attractive force toward goal.
- $\mathbf{F}_{rep} = $ repulsive force from each nearby obstacle (inversely proportional to squared distance, active within $\rho_0$).
- $\mathbf{F}_{total} = \mathbf{F}_{att} + \sum_i \mathbf{F}_{rep,i}$.
- Robot follows the negative gradient: $q_{new} = q_{old} + \eta \cdot \mathbf{F}_{total}$.

In **dynamic** environments, the obstacle list $O(t)$ is updated at each control cycle from sensor data.

**Local Minima Problem (2 marks):**

A local minimum occurs when $\mathbf{F}_{total} = \mathbf{0}$ at some $q_{min} \ne q_{goal}$. The robot stalls indefinitely. Common scenarios:
- Robot aligned with an obstacle, equidistant from goal — attractive and repulsive forces cancel.
- Robot trapped in a U-shaped obstacle concavity.

**Two Cloud-Assisted Escape Strategies (2 marks):**

1. **Random Walk / Random Restart (Perturbation Strategy):** The cloud detects the stall (via velocity $\approx 0$ for $>T_{stall}$ seconds) and injects a random direction $\mathbf{v}_{rand} \in \mathcal{S}^{n-1}$ for $\eta$ seconds, breaking the force equilibrium.

2. **Virtual Wall / Wall-Following Heuristic:** The cloud commands the robot to follow the obstacle boundary (tangential motion) until $\mathbf{F}_{att}$ re-aligns with a feasible descent direction, then resumes normal potential-field motion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often only mention "add a random force" without specifying the **detection criterion** (stall detection) or the **recovery supervisor** (cloud role). Examiners expect you to mention *how the cloud knows* the robot is stuck and *what computational intelligence* it provides for recovery.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ **Navigation Planning** = Global + Local + Replanning layers in cloud robotics.
- ☐ **Hierarchical split is mandatory:** Cloud does search, edge does safety.
- ☐ **A\* formula:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — memorize exactly.
- ☐ **Admissible heuristic** $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ $\Rightarrow$ A* returns **optimal** path.
- ☐ **Manhattan** for 4-connected grids; **Euclidean/Octile** for 8-connected.
- ☐ **Dijkstra** = A* with $h(n) = 0$.
- ☐ **D\* Lite** = incremental A* for **dynamic replanning** in changing maps.
- ☐ **RRT** is *probabilistically complete* but *not optimal*; **RRT\*** is *asymptotically optimal*.
- ☐ **Potential Field** is fast and reactive but suffers from **local minima**.
- ☐ **Local minima escapes:** random walk, wall-following, virtual obstacles.
- ☐ **Cloud RTT budget:** $t_{total} = t_{uplink} + t_{plan} + t_{downlink} \le t_{deadline}$.
- ☐ **Speedup factor** $S = t_{onboard}/t_{cloud}$ — typical values 3–10×.
- ☐ **SLAM-Navigation coupling:** Cloud SLAM feeds world model → planner → controller.
- ☐ **Multi-robot coordination** is uniquely enabled by the *shared cloud map*.
- ☐ **Safety override:** Local reactive layer must always have authority to override cloud commands.
- ☐ **Common 14-mark mistakes:** Forgetting admissibility proof, ignoring dynamic replanning, not mentioning local-minima recovery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Cloud for thought, edge for reflex."* If you remember this one phrase, you can frame 80% of the architecture and algorithm questions correctly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT866 - CLOUD ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping (SLAM)** is a computational problem in robotics and autonomous systems that involves constructing or updating a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously keeping track of an agent's (robot's) location within that environment. Formally, SLAM is a recursive Bayesian estimation problem that seeks to compute the posterior probability distribution over the robot's trajectory and the map's landmark positions, given the sequence of control inputs and sensor observations up to time $t$.

In the context of **Cloud Robotics** (PERUT866), SLAM is treated as a computational workload that can be partitioned between the on-board robot (edge layer) and the remote cloud infrastructure, exploiting the latter's massive parallel compute (GPUs/CPUs) to perform heavy map fusion, pose graph optimization, and large-scale data association in near real-time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERUT866, Module 2):**
> SLAM is a foundational perception algorithm in cloud robotics. The student is expected to articulate the algorithmic pipeline, the probabilistic formulation, and the rationale for offloading SLAM sub-tasks to the cloud (e.g., map storage, multi-robot map merging, deep-learning-based loop closure).

## 1.2 The Problem SLAM Solves — The "Chicken-and-Egg" Dilemma

To localise itself, a robot needs an accurate map.
To build a map, the robot needs to know its own accurate pose.
Both are unknown — hence the **chicken-and-egg** dependency.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> **Analogy: Exploring a Dark Museum with a Flashlight**
>
> Imagine you wake up blindfolded in a pitch-dark museum. You hold a flashlight that can only illuminate a small circle 2 meters wide.
> - **Localisation** = remembering where *you* are standing (your internal sense of position).
> - **Mapping** = remembering the shape and location of each statue (the environment).
> - **SLAM** = the moment you take a step, the flashlight reveals new statues, but you only know your step length, not your *direction* precisely. You use the *newly seen statues* to correct where you think you are, and you use *where you think you are* to place the new statues correctly in your mental map.
> - **Cloud Offload** = shouting into a walkie-talkie to a friend in a control room who has seen the *entire* museum floor plan on a CCTV monitor. Your friend tells you, "You are at statue #14, and the next statue is 3 m to your North-East." You correct your map and your location simultaneously using this globally-aware information.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in SLAM

- **ATE (Absolute Trajectory Error)** — Root mean square error between estimated and ground-truth trajectory, expressed in **metres (m)**.
- **RPE (Relative Pose Error)** — Drift over fixed time/distance intervals.
- **Map Coverage** — Percentage of environment reconstructed.
- **CPU/RAM footprint** — Critical for cloud offload decisions (GB of RAM, GFLOPS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Cloud Robotics Tie-In:** On-board SLAM typically consumes **5–15 GB RAM** and **20–50 W** of power. Offloading to the cloud reduces on-board power draw to under **2 W**, enabling long-duration missions for aerial and field robots — a key talking point in KTU viva voce.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probabilistic Formulation of SLAM

SLAM is posed as estimating the joint posterior:

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where:
- $x_{1:t} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_t\}$ — robot pose trajectory.
- $m = \{m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n\}$ — map of $n$ landmarks.
- $z_{1:t}$ — sequence of sensor observations.
- $u_{1:t}$ — sequence of control inputs (odometry).

The full SLAM problem estimates the *entire trajectory and map*, while the **online SLAM** problem marginalises out past poses:

$$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

## 2.2 The SLAM Algorithm — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Prediction (Motion Update):** Propagate the robot's pose estimate forward using the motion model $x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$ where $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ is the process noise.
2. **Observation (Sensor Read):** Acquire sensor measurements $z_t$ of nearby landmarks using the observation model $z_t = h(x_t) + v_t$ where $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ is the measurement noise.
3. **Data Association:** Match current observations $z_t$ to previously mapped landmarks (or instantiate new landmarks). The Nearest-Neighbour (NN) or Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound (JCBB) test is used.
4. **Measurement Update (Correction):** Use the difference between expected and actual observations (innovation $\nu_t$) to correct the pose and map estimates via the Kalman gain $K_t$.
5. **Map Augmentation:** Add new landmark estimates to the state vector and covariance matrix.
6. **Loop Closure (optional but critical):** When the robot revisits a known area, a global correction is applied — in cloud robotics, this is often offloaded to a server running **Pose Graph Optimisation (PGO)** with tools like **g2o** or **GTSAM**.

## 2.3 Variants of SLAM (KTU High-Yield)

| Variant | Sensor | Core Algorithm | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EKF-SLAM** | Range-bearing (LiDAR, sonar) | Extended Kalman Filter | Small maps, 2D |
| **FastSLAM** | Range-bearing | Rao-Blackwellised Particle Filter | Medium maps |
| **Graph-SLAM** | Any | Pose Graph Optimisation | Large maps, offline |
| **Visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono)** | Mono/Stereo camera | Feature-based + Bundle Adjustment | Indoor, low-cost |
| **LIO-SAM** | LiDAR + IMU | Factor Graph + iSAM2 | Autonomous vehicles |
| **Cartographer** | 2D/3D LiDAR | Submap matching + PGO | Google-grade indoor maps |

## 2.4 The "Why" — Why Offload SLAM to the Cloud?

- **Compute Bottleneck:** EKF-SLAM's covariance matrix is $O(N^2)$ where $N$ is the number of landmarks. Beyond $N \approx 10^4$, the matrix inversion cost becomes prohibitive for on-board CPUs.
- **Multi-Robot Collaboration:** Cloud acts as a central map server — robots upload local submaps; the cloud performs **map fusion** using techniques like **Distributed Consistent Pose Estimation (DCPE)**.
- **Deep Learning for Place Recognition:** Cloud can run a CNN (e.g., NetVLAD) to perform loop closure across all robots in a fleet.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Online SLAM posterior | Recursive Bayesian formulation |
| 2 | $\bar{\mu}_t = f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Predicted mean state | Motion model $f$ |
| 3 | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = F_t \Sigma_{t-1} F_t^\top + Q_t$ | Predicted covariance | $F_t$ is motion Jacobian |
| 4 | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + R_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | $H_t$ is observation Jacobian |
| 5 | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$ | Updated state | Innovation $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ |
| 6 | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Updated covariance | Joseph form also acceptable |
| 7 | $S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + R_t$ | Innovation covariance | Used for data association gate |
| 8 | $N\!N$ Test: $D^2 = \nu_t^\top S_t^{-1} \nu_t < \chi^2_{d,\alpha}$ | Mahalanobis gating | $d$ = measurement dimension |
| 9 | $\text{ATE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{t}\sum_{i=1}^{t}\vert \vert \text{trans}(x_i^{\text{est}}) - \text{trans}(x_i^{\text{gt}}) \vert \vert^2}$ | Absolute Trajectory Error | In **metres (m)** |
| 10 | $F_t = \left.\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right\vert_{\mu_{t-1},u_t}$ | Motion Jacobian | Linearisation of $f$ |
| 11 | $H_t = \left.\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ | Observation Jacobian | Linearisation of $h$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations form the bedrock of every modern SLAM library (GTSAM, OpenSLAM, MRPT). In the cloud, the matrix inversion in Equation 4 is parallelised using **CUDA** or **MPI**, reducing per-update latency from **~800 ms (on-board)** to **~40 ms (cloud GPU)** for $N = 5000$ landmarks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EKF-SLAM Prediction Step

We start with the motion model for a differential-drive robot operating in 2D:

$$x_t = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{t-1} + \Delta s \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta/2) \\ y_{t-1} + \Delta s \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta/2) \\ \theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta \end{bmatrix} + w_t$$

where $\Delta s$ and $\Delta \theta$ are the odometry-derived arc length and heading change.

**Step 1 — State prediction (mean):**

$$\bar{\mu}_t = f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) = \mu_{t-1} + \begin{bmatrix} \Delta s \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta/2) \\ \Delta s \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta/2) \\ \Delta \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

This simply integrates the odometry to obtain a prior estimate of the new pose.

**Step 2 — Linearisation (compute motion Jacobian $F_t$):**

$$F_t = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\Delta s \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta/2) \\ 0 & 1 & \;\;\Delta s \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta/2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The third column arises from differentiating $\cos$ and $\sin$ with respect to $\theta$.

**Step 3 — Covariance prediction:**

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = F_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, F_t^\top + Q_t$$

The covariance is propagated using the linearised dynamics plus the process-noise covariance $Q_t$ (modelling wheel slip, floor irregularities, etc.).

## 3.2 Derivation of the EKF-SLAM Observation Model

The robot observes landmark $j$ with range $r_{t,j}$ and bearing $\phi_{t,j}$:

$$z_{t,j} = h(x_t, m_j) + v_t = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(m_{j,x} - x)^2 + (m_{j,y} - y)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_{j,y} - y,\; m_{j,x} - x) - \theta \end{bmatrix} + v_t$$

**Step 4 — Innovation (measurement residual):**

$$\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$$

This is the difference between what the sensor reported and what the model predicted. If the SLAM estimate were perfect, $\nu_t$ would be pure noise.

**Step 5 — Innovation covariance:**

$$S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + R_t$$

**Step 6 — Observation Jacobian $H_t$:**

Define $\delta_x = m_{j,x} - x$, $\delta_y = m_{j,y} - y$, and $q = \delta_x^2 + \delta_y^2$.

$$H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -\delta_x / \sqrt{q} & -\delta_y / \sqrt{q} & 0 \\ \delta_y / q & -\delta_x / q & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \end{bmatrix}$$

The first two rows differentiate the range and bearing w.r.t. pose; remaining rows (zero) w.r.t. other landmark states.

**Step 7 — Kalman gain:**

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top S_t^{-1}$$

**Step 8 — Posterior update:**

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

**Step 9 — Augment with new landmark (if $z_t$ does not match any known landmark):**

The new landmark's mean is initialised by inverting $h$:

$$m_{\text{new}} = \begin{bmatrix} x + r \cos(\theta + \phi) \\ y + r \sin(\theta + \phi) \end{bmatrix}$$

The covariance block is appended using the chain rule:

$$\Sigma_{aug} = \begin{bmatrix} \Sigma_t & \Sigma_t G^\top \\ G \Sigma_t & G \Sigma_t G^\top + R_t \end{bmatrix}$$

where $G = \partial h^{-1} / \partial z$ is the inverse-observation Jacobian.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Input | Operation | Output | Cost (FLOPs) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu_{t-1}, u_t$ | $f(\cdot)$ | $\bar{\mu}_t$ | $O(1)$ |
| 2 | $f, \mu_{t-1}$ | Jacobian $\partial f/\partial x$ | $F_t$ | $O(1)$ |
| 3 | $\Sigma_{t-1}, F_t, Q_t$ | $F\Sigma F^\top + Q$ | $\bar{\Sigma}_t$ | $O(N^2)$ |
| 4 | $z_t, h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ | $z - h(\bar{\mu})$ | $\nu_t$ | $O(M)$ |
| 5 | $H_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t, R_t$ | $H\Sigma H^\top + R$ | $S_t$ | $O(N^2)$ |
| 6 | $H_t$ | Jacobian $\partial h/\partial x$ | $H_t$ | $O(1)$ |
| 7 | $\bar{\Sigma}_t, H_t, S_t$ | $\Sigma H^\top S^{-1}$ | $K_t$ | $O(N^3)$ |
| 8 | $\bar{\mu}_t, K_t, \nu_t$ | $\bar{\mu} + K\nu$ | $\mu_t$ | $O(N)$ |
| 9 | $\mu_t, \Sigma_t, m_{\text{new}}$ | Augment | $\mu_{t+1}, \Sigma_{t+1}$ | $O(N^2)$ |

where $N$ = state dimension and $M$ = number of detected features per scan.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Offloading Insight (KTU Hot Topic):** The dominant cost is the $O(N^3)$ matrix inversion in Step 7 and the $O(N^2)$ updates in Steps 3 and 9. For $N = 10^4$, on-board CPU takes **>1 s** per update; a cloud GPU using cuBLAS completes the same step in **~15 ms**, justifying the cloud-robotics SLAM architecture.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

Per the protocol, every node ID is alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, and free of reserved keywords. Labels are clean, uppercase alphanumeric, with no markdown formatting tags.

## 4.2 SLAM Functional Architecture Flow (Edge + Cloud)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[ROBOT EDGE LAYER]:::edge
    A1[SENSORS CAMERA LIDAR IMU WHEEL ODOMETRY]:::sensor
    A2[LOCAL EKF PREDICTION STEP]:::edgeproc
    A3[FEATURE EXTRACTION ORB FAST LOAM]:::edgeproc
    A4[WIRELESS LINK 4G 5G WIFI 6]:::cloudlink
    B0[CLOUD INFRASTRUCTURE LAYER]:::cloud
    B1[MAP FUSION SERVER]:::cloudproc
    B2[LOOP CLOSURE NETVLAD CNN]:::cloudproc
    B3[POSE GRAPH OPTIMIZATION G2O GTSAM]:::cloudproc
    B4[SHARED GLOBAL MAP DATABASE REDIS POSTGRES]:::clouddb
    C0[ROBOT DECISION LAYER]:::decision
    C1[PATH PLANNER A STAR RRT]:::decision
    C2[MOTOR CONTROLLER PID MPC]:::decision

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A1 --> A3
    A2 --> A4
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> A4
    A4 --> C0
    C0 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> A1

    classDef edge fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef edgeproc fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    classDef cloud fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#000
    classDef cloudproc fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    classDef clouddb fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207,color:#000
    classDef cloudlink fill:#c7d2fe,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
```

## 4.3 EKF-SLAM Inner Loop — Prediction / Update Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[START TIME T]:::startnode
    S2[READ ODOMETRY U T]:::datain
    S3[PREDICT POSE X T]:::predict
    S4[READ SENSOR Z T]:::datain
    S5[DATA ASSOCIATION NN JCBB]:::assoc
    S6{IS MATCH FOUND}:::decisionnode
    S7[UPDATE WITH KALMAN GAIN K T]:::update
    S8[ADD NEW LANDMARK TO MAP]:::update
    S9[CHECK FOR LOOP CLOSURE]:::loopnode
    S10[PUBLISH ESTIMATE X T MAP T]:::outputnode
    S11[END INCREMENT T TO T PLUS 1]:::startnode

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6
    S6 -- YES --> S7 --> S9
    S6 -- NO --> S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10 --> S11

    classDef startnode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef datain fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef predict fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    classDef assoc fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,color:#000
    classDef decisionnode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207,color:#000
    classDef update fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    classDef loopnode fill:#c7d2fe,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000
    classDef outputnode fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Reading the Diagrams

- **Diagram 4.2** shows the **horizontal partitioning** of SLAM: lightweight EKF prediction stays on the robot; heavy loop closure, pose graph optimisation, and multi-robot map fusion live in the cloud.
- **Diagram 4.3** shows the **vertical time-evolution** of one SLAM tick — the *predict–measure–associate–update* cycle that repeats at the sensor rate (typically **10–50 Hz** for LiDAR, **30–60 Hz** for camera).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**Define the SLAM problem. Why is it considered a "chicken-and-egg" problem in robotics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping is the computational problem of estimating a robot's pose $x_t$ and the map $m$ of its environment from a stream of control inputs $u_{1:t}$ and sensor observations $z_{1:t}$, formalised as computing the posterior $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
- **Chicken-and-Egg Explanation (1 mark):** Accurate localisation requires an accurate map, while accurate map-building requires accurate localisation. SLAM resolves this circular dependency by jointly estimating both using probabilistic filtering.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**List any three SLAM variants and state one distinguishing feature of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per variant):**
1. **EKF-SLAM** — uses Extended Kalman Filter; suitable for small maps (<1000 landmarks); covariance grows quadratically.
2. **FastSLAM** — uses Rao-Blackwellised particle filter; each particle maintains an independent EKF per landmark; scales to medium maps.
3. **Visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM2)** — uses camera features (ORB); enables low-cost deployment; supports monocular, stereo, and RGB-D.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

**(a)** Derive the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) prediction and update equations for a 2D SLAM system. Clearly define the state vector, motion model, observation model, motion Jacobian $F_t$, and observation Jacobian $H_t$. State the role of the process noise covariance $Q_t$ and measurement noise covariance $R_t$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a block diagram how a Cloud Robotics architecture offloads heavy SLAM sub-tasks (map fusion, loop closure, pose graph optimisation) from the on-board robot to the cloud. Justify this offload using computational complexity arguments and discuss any one trade-off. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a) — EKF-SLAM Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State vector definition (1 mark):**

The augmented state vector contains the robot pose followed by the Cartesian positions of $n$ mapped landmarks:

$$\mu_t = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \\ m_{1,x} \\ m_{1,y} \\ \vdots \\ m_{n,x} \\ m_{n,y} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mu_t \in \mathbb{R}^{(3 + 2n)}$$

**Step 2 — Motion model (1 mark):**

$$x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t, \quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$

For a differential-drive robot using odometry $u_t = [\Delta s, \Delta \theta]^\top$:

$$f(x_{t-1}, u_t) = \begin{bmatrix} x_{t-1} + \Delta s \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta/2) \\ y_{t-1} + \Delta s \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta/2) \\ \theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

Landmarks do not move, so the rest of $f$ is identity.

**Step 3 — Motion Jacobian $F_t$ (1 mark):**

$$F_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\Delta s \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta/2) \\ 0 & 1 & \;\;\Delta s \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta/2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ & \vdots & \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{(3+2n) \times 3}$$

with the rest of the matrix being identity for the landmark positions.

**Step 4 — Covariance prediction (1 mark):**

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = F_t \Sigma_{t-1} F_t^\top + Q_t$$

$Q_t$ models wheel slip, floor irregularities, and IMU drift. $Q_t$ is a $3 \times 3$ block inserted at the top-left.

**Step 5 — Observation model (1 mark):**

$$z_{t,j} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{t,j} \\ \phi_{t,j} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(m_{j,x}-x)^2 + (m_{j,y}-y)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_{j,y}-y,\; m_{j,x}-x) - \theta \end{bmatrix} + v_t, \quad v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

**Step 6 — Update equations (2 marks):**

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + R_t)^{-1}$$

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$$

The term $H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + R_t$ is the **innovation covariance** $S_t$. The Kalman gain $K_t$ optimally weights the innovation against prior uncertainty.

---

#### Solution to A(b) — Cloud Offload Architecture (7 marks)

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

Refer to **Diagram 4.2** in Section 4. The robot's edge layer performs:
- Sensor data acquisition (LiDAR, camera, IMU, wheel odometry).
- Local EKF prediction step.
- Feature extraction (ORB, FAST, LOAM).

The cloud layer performs:
- Map fusion across multiple robots.
- Deep-learning-based loop closure (e.g., NetVLAD CNN).
- Pose Graph Optimisation using g2o or GTSAM.
- Persistent global map storage.

Communication occurs over a 5G / Wi-Fi-6 link with JSON / Protobuf messages.

**Computational Complexity Justification (2 marks):**

| Operation | Edge (on-board CPU) | Cloud (GPU) |
|---|---|---|
| Covariance update $F\Sigma F^\top$ | $O(N^2)$ ≈ **2 s** for $N = 5000$ | $O(N^2)$ ≈ **30 ms** |
| Kalman gain inversion | $O(N^3)$ ≈ **1.5 s** | $O(N^3)$ ≈ **15 ms** |
| Loop closure CNN inference | **~400 ms** (CPU) | **~12 ms** (V100 GPU) |

**Trade-off (2 marks):**
- **+ Latency reduction** for compute-heavy steps; **+ multi-robot map sharing**; **+ reduced on-board power**.
- **− Latency from network round-trip** (5G: 10–20 ms; Wi-Fi: 5–50 ms); **− reliance on connectivity** (fails in GPS-denied / underground / underwater scenarios); **− data privacy** if maps contain sensitive information.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** Compare EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, and Graph-SLAM in terms of computational complexity, scalability, and the type of sensor data they accept. State one practical application of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the role of data association and loop closure in SLAM. Explain the Mahalanobis distance-based nearest-neighbour test for data association and derive the condition under which a measurement is accepted as matching a known landmark. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a) — Comparison (7 marks)

| Property | EKF-SLAM | FastSLAM | Graph-SLAM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Filter type** | Single EKF | Particle filter + per-landmark EKF | Pose graph optimisation |
| **Complexity** | $O(N^2)$ update, $O(N^3)$ inversion | $O(M \log N)$ per particle | $O(N^3)$ for sparse solve |
| **Scalability** | Poor above $N \approx 10^3$ | Moderate, $M$ particles | Excellent (sparse solvers) |
| **Sensor data** | Range-bearing (LiDAR, sonar) | Range-bearing, 2D maps | Any (LiDAR, camera, GPS) |
| **Practical application** | Indoor robot vacuums | Hobbyist drones (ROS) | Autonomous vehicles (LIO-SAM) |
| **Strength** | Mathematically rigorous | Handles multi-modal posteriors | Optimal full trajectory estimate |
| **Weakness** | Quadratic covariance growth | Particle deprivation | Requires good initial guess |

**Rubric (1 mark per row, max 5; +2 marks for correct application examples).**

---

#### Solution to B(b) — Data Association and Loop Closure (7 marks)

**Data Association (3 marks):**
- *Definition:* The process of matching newly observed features to previously mapped landmarks.
- *Why critical:* Wrong associations introduce large errors and corrupt the map.
- *NN test using Mahalanobis distance:*

$$D^2 = \nu_t^\top S_t^{-1} \nu_t$$

where $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ is the innovation and $S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + R_t$ is the innovation covariance.

**Derivation of acceptance condition (2 marks):**

If both $\nu_t$ and the measurement noise are Gaussian, then $D^2$ follows a chi-squared distribution with $d$ degrees of freedom ($d = 2$ for 2D range-bearing).

$$D^2 \sim \chi^2_d$$

We accept the association if:

$$D^2 < \chi^2_{d, \alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ is the confidence level (typically $0.05$, giving $\chi^2_{2, 0.05} = 5.99$).

**Loop Closure (2 marks):**
- *Definition:* Recognition that the robot has returned to a previously visited location.
- *Effect:* Triggers a global correction using all historical pose constraints, dramatically reducing accumulated drift.
- *In Cloud Robotics:* A CNN (e.g., NetVLAD, DELF) computes image descriptors and matches against a global descriptor index in the cloud; matched places are returned to the robot for graph-based optimisation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in SLAM Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the state-augmentation step** when a new landmark is detected — examiners specifically look for the Jacobian of the inverse observation model (Step 9 of Section 3.2). Loss: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing online vs. full SLAM posteriors** — $p(x_t, m \mid \ldots)$ vs. $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid \ldots)$. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Not expressing the chi-squared gating threshold numerically** — always quote $\chi^2_{2, 0.05} = 5.99$ or similar explicit value, not just "use a threshold." Loss: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping the process noise $Q_t$ and measurement noise $R_t$ definitions** — these are mandatory for full marks on the EKF derivation.
> 5. **In cloud offloading answers, omitting the trade-off (latency, network failure, privacy)** — a one-sided pro-cloud answer is graded at most **4/7** for the offload question.
> 6. **Writing Jacobians $F_t$ and $H_t$ without evaluating them at the operating point** — examiners expect $\partial f / \partial x \vert_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t}$, not bare symbols.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SLAM = joint estimation of pose and map**, formalised by the posterior $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
- The **chicken-and-egg problem** is resolved by recursive Bayesian estimation (Kalman / Particle / Graph).
- **EKF-SLAM** is foundational: it has $O(N^2)$ covariance and $O(N^3)$ inversion cost — the reason cloud offload is attractive.
- **State vector** = robot pose + all landmark positions; new landmarks are added via **inverse observation** with Jacobian $G$.
- **Data association** is gated by the **Mahalanobis distance** test using the chi-squared distribution.
- **Loop closure** corrects accumulated drift and is best performed globally — naturally suited to **cloud offload**.
- **Cloud Robotics SLAM architecture** partitions: edge = predict + extract features; cloud = fuse maps, close loops, optimise pose graph.
- **Key metrics** for evaluation: **ATE (m)**, **RPE**, and **map coverage (%)**.
- **Variants to remember**: EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, Graph-SLAM, ORB-SLAM2, LIO-SAM, Cartographer.
- **Trade-off triangle** of cloud offload: **latency ↓, scalability ↑, energy ↓, but connectivity ↑ required**.
- **Equations to memorise cold**: motion update, Jacobian $F_t$, Kalman gain $K_t$, state update $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t$, and innovation covariance $S_t$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Operating Systems and Its Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT866 - CLOUD ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Robot Operating Systems and Its Architecture # 🟢 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Robot Operating Systems (ROS) ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Robot Operating System (ROS)** is an open-source, meta-operating system (or *middleware framework*) for robots. It provides the structured communication layer on top of a conventional host operating system (typically Ubuntu Linux). ROS offers hardware abstraction, device drivers, libraries, visualizers, message-passing, and package management — making it the de-facto *software backbone* of modern robotics research and Industry 4.0 deployments. ROS was originally developed at **Stanford Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (SAIL)** in 2007 under the name *Switchyard*, and its maintenance was later taken over by **Open Source Robotics Foundation (OSRF)** in 2013. It is licensed under the permissive **BSD (Berkeley Software Distribution)** license, allowing unrestricted commercial and academic use. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — ROS as the "Post Office of a Robot":** > Imagine a robot as a **busy city** with many specialized workers: one reads the camera, another plans the path, a third controls the motors, and a fourth streams LiDAR data. Without a postal system, these workers cannot talk to each other. **ROS acts as that postal system** — a *structured messaging infrastructure* where every worker (called a **Node**) can *publish* letters (messages) into a *mailbox* (called a **Topic**), and any other worker can *subscribe* to that mailbox. The Postmaster General is the **ROS Master**, who keeps the directory of all mailboxes. ## 1.3 Core Design Philosophy | Design Goal | Engineering Rationale | |---|---| | **Peer-to-Peer** | Eliminates single-point failure; nodes are loosely coupled | | **Multi-Language** | Supports C++, Python, Java, MATLAB, Lua, Lisp | | **Thin & Tools-Based** | Drivers and algorithms are standalone libraries without ROS dependency | | **Free & Open Source** | Fosters global research collaboration; BSD license permits commercial use | | **Modular & Distributed** | Computation can be offloaded to multiple CPUs, GPUs, or cloud servers | ## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Default middleware (ROS 1):** XML-RPC + TCPROS (custom protocol) > - **Default middleware (ROS 2):** **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** — an OMG standard > - **Default port of ROS Master:** `11311` (TCP) > - **Default file extensions:** `.msg` (messages), `.srv` (services), `.action` (actions) > - **Canonical release line (KTU 2024):** **ROS Noetic Ninjemys (ROS 1)** & **ROS 2 Humble Hawksbill** (LTS) > - **Canonical hardware abstraction rate:** up to **1000 Hz** message throughput for TCPROS, **> 10 kHz** with UDPROS ## 1.5 Why ROS Matters for Cloud Robotics In **Cloud Robotics**, computation is offloaded from the physical robot to remote cloud servers. ROS naturally supports this because: 1. Its peer-to-peer architecture is **network-transparent**. 2. Topics, services, and actions can be transparently routed across **LAN, Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, or VPN**. 3. Tools like **`rosbridge_suite`** allow non-ROS clients (e.g., browsers, mobile apps) to interact with ROS systems over **WebSockets**. # 🟡 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Three-Layered ROS Architecture ROS architecture is canonically partitioned into **three distinct levels** (or domains). Each level serves a separate audience with separate concerns. --- ## 2.1 🏛️ Level 1 — The Filesystem Level *(What the developer sees on disk)* > [!NOTE] > This level describes how ROS code is **organized on the developer's hard drive** — packages, manifests, message definitions, and build artifacts. | Component | File / Directory | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Package** | A folder containing `package.xml` + `CMakeLists.txt` | The **atomic unit** of ROS; smallest thing you can build & release | | **Metapackage** | A package that groups related packages (e.g., `navigation`, `moveit`) | Logical grouping for distribution | | **Package Manifest** | `package.xml` | XML metadata: name, version, author, **dependencies** (``, ``) | | **Message Definition** | `msg/MyMessage.msg` | Defines the **data structure** of a message (fields + types) | | **Service Definition** | `srv/MyService.srv` | Defines the **request/response** schema | | **Action Definition** | `action/MyAction.action` | Defines **goal / result / feedback** schema for long-running tasks | | **Repository** | A version-controlled collection of packages (e.g., on GitHub) | Source of truth for distributed teams | | **ROS Distribution** | A versioned set of compatible packages (e.g., `Noetic`) | Snapshot used in production | ### Example: Message File A `.msg` file is a plain text definition of fields separated by whitespace: ``` # Point.msg float64 x float64 y float64 z ``` The build system (`catkin` for ROS 1, `colcon` for ROS 2) auto-generates source code bindings for all 8 supported languages from these files. --- ## 2.2 ⚙️ Level 2 — The Computation Graph Level *(What runs in memory when `roscore` starts)* This is the **runtime peer-to-peer network of ROS processes**. All data flow at runtime happens here. | Node / Element | Symbol | Role | |---|---|---| | **Node** | `N_i` | Executable process using ROS client library (`roscpp`, `rospy`, `rclcpp`, `rclpy`) | | **ROS Master** | `M` | Name service: enables nodes to **find each other** (does **not** route messages itself) | | **Parameter Server** | `P` | Centralized key-value store for configuration (`/robot/max_velocity := 1.5`) | | **Topic** | `T` | Named bus over which nodes **asynchronously exchange messages** (pub/sub) | | **Message** | `m` | Strictly-typed data packet published to a topic | | **Service** | `S` | Synchronous **request/reply** RPC mechanism (1 client ↔ 1 server) | | **Action** | `A` | Long-running task with **goal + feedback + result** (used by `move_base`) | | **Bag** | `B` | File format for **recording & replaying** message streams (`.bag`) | | **Connection** | Edge between two nodes | Carries serialized messages via **TCPROS** or **UDPROS** | ### Communication Paradigms (The Heart of ROS) > [!IMPORTANT] > ROS offers **three primary inter-process communication paradigms**: #### A. Publish / Subscribe (Asynchronous, Many-to-Many) - **One-way**, **non-blocking**, **anonymous** data stream. - Used for: sensor streams (camera, LiDAR), odometry, motor commands. #### B. Service / Client (Synchronous, One-to-One) - **Two-way**, **blocking** remote procedure call. - Used for: configuration, triggering an action, getting a snapshot. #### C. Action / Server (Asynchronous, with Feedback) - **Two-way** but **non-blocking**, with **continuous feedback**. - Used for: navigation, manipulation — anything that takes seconds to minutes. ### Mathematical Model of Pub/Sub Throughput Let $N_p$ be the number of publishers, $N_s$ the subscribers, $\lambda$ the message rate (Hz), $S_m$ the message size (bytes). Then the **aggregate topic bandwidth** $B_T$ (bytes/sec) is given by: $$ B_T = N_p \cdot \lambda \cdot S_m \quad \text{(publish side)} $$ $$ B_{\text{total}} = N_s \cdot B_T = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot \lambda \cdot S_m \quad \text{(network)} $$ For a typical camera at $30\,\text{Hz}$ with $1\,\text{MB}$ frames and 2 subscribers: $$ B_{\text{total}} = 2 \cdot 1 \cdot 30 \cdot 1{,}048{,}576 = 62.9\,\text{MB/s} $$ This is why ROS pushes researchers to use **compressed image topics** (`/camera/image_raw/compressed`) and **theora streams** for cloud offloading. --- ## 2.3 🌍 Level 3 — The Community Level *(How the global ecosystem is organized)* | Component | Description | |---|---| | **Distributions** | Versioned collections of compatible packages — e.g., `Noetic`, `Humble`, `Iron`, `Jazzy` | | **Repositories** | Network-distributed code sources (e.g., `github.com/ros/...`) | | **ROS Wiki** | `wiki.ros.org` — primary documentation site | | **ROS Answers** | `answers.ros.org` — Stack-Overflow-style Q&A | | **ROS Discourse** | `discourse.ros.org` — community announcements | | **Index** | `index.ros.org` — searchable package catalog | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Key formulas every KTU student must memorize for the cloud robotics paper:** | # | Concept | Formula | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Topic bandwidth | $B_T = N_p \cdot \lambda \cdot S_m$ | Single subscriber side | | 2 | Network throughput | $B_{\text{total}} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot \lambda \cdot S_m$ | Total wire load | | 3 | End-to-end latency (cloud offload) | $T_{e2e} = T_{proc} + T_{net} + T_{queue}$ | Sum of compute + network + broker delay | | 4 | Round-trip time (service) | $T_{RTT} = 2 \cdot T_{prop} + 2 \cdot T_{trans} + T_{proc}$ | Photon / serial transit | | 5 | Master lookup probability (P2P) | $P_{\text{conn}} = 1 - (1 - p)^{N_{\text{retry}}}$ | Bernoulli retry model | | 6 | TF transform rate (typical) | $30$–$100\,\text{Hz}$ | Required for control loops | | 7 | ROS Master default port | $11311 / \text{TCP}$ | XML-RPC registry | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Substitution rule for math tables:** When expressing a magnitude (e.g., absolute value of $-3$), use the LaTeX command `\vert -3 \vert` instead of `|−3|`. This prevents the markdown table parser from breaking the layout. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility ROS is the **backbone of**: - **Autonomous Vehicles** — Apollo (Baidu), Autoware (Tier IV) - **Industrial Arms** — Universal Robots, Franka Emika Panda (uses `franka_ros`) - **Service Robots** — SoftBank Pepper, Fetch Robotics Freight - **Cloud Robotics R&D** — Google Robotics Cloud, AWS RoboMaker, **Rapyuta** (the foundational cloud robotics platform) - **NASA** — NASA's **VIPER lunar rover** uses ROS 2 for autonomy # 🔵 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Message Flow & TF Kinematics ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: End-to-End Latency in a Cloud-Robot Topic Pipeline When a sensor message is published onboard the robot and consumed by a cloud-hosted algorithm, the message traverses **six distinct time-delays**. Deriving this rigorously is essential for KTU exam-style problems. > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** A mobile robot publishes LiDAR scans at $40\,\text{Hz}$ (message size $S_m = 50\,\text{kB}$). The subscriber (an SLAM node) runs on a cloud server $200\,\text{km}$ away over a fiber link. Compute the end-to-end latency. ### Step 1 — Identify the delay components A topic message from sensor to cloud consumer passes through: $$ T_{e2e} = T_{\text{pub}} + T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{broker}} + T_{\text{sub}} $$ where each term represents a distinct sub-process. ### Step 2 — Publisher serialization delay The publisher must serialize the message (CDR encoding in ROS 2 / DDS, or custom ROS 1 wire format). This scales with CPU clock and message size. $$ T_{\text{serial}} = \frac{S_m}{R_{\text{serial}}} $$ Assuming a modern CPU at $R_{\text{serial}} = 500\,\text{MB/s}$ for CDR encoding and $S_m = 50{,}000$ bytes: $$ T_{\text{serial}} = \frac{50{,}000}{500 \times 10^{6}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 0.1\,\text{ms} $$ ### Step 3 — Transmission delay (putting bits on the wire) If the link rate is $R_{\text{link}} = 1\,\text{Gbps} = 1.25 \times 10^{8}\,\text{bytes/s}$: $$ T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{S_m}{R_{\text{link}}} = \frac{50{,}000}{1.25 \times 10^{8}} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 0.4\,\text{ms} $$ ### Step 4 — Propagation delay (speed of light in fiber) Fiber speed is roughly $2 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$ (≈ 2/3 of vacuum $c$). Distance $d = 200{,}000\,\text{m}$: $$ T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{v_{\text{fiber}}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{5}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1.0\,\text{ms} $$ ### Step 5 — Queueing / broker delay (DDS discovery) In DDS, the data is typically relayed by a discovery service. For a lightly loaded network (utilization $\rho < 0.5$), M/M/1 queueing theory gives: $$ T_{\text{queue}} = \frac{\rho / \mu}{1 - \rho} $$ where $\rho = \lambda / \mu$. With $\lambda = 40\,\text{Hz}$, $\mu = 1000\,\text{Hz}$ server capacity, $\rho = 0.04$: $$ T_{\text{queue}} = \frac{0.04 / 1000}{1 - 0.04} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-5}}{0.96} = 4.17 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{s} = 0.042\,\text{ms} $$ ### Step 6 — Publish / subscribe scheduling jitter Operating-system scheduling introduces $T_{\text{pub}} = T_{\text{sub}} = 0.5\,\text{ms}$ each (assuming a 1 kHz scheduler). ### Step 7 — Total end-to-end latency Summing all six contributions: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{e2e} &= T_{\text{pub}} + T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{broker}} + T_{\text{sub}} \\ &= 0.5 + 0.1 + 1.0 + 0.4 + 0.042 + 0.5 \\ &= 2.542\,\text{ms} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 8 — Verify against hard real-time constraints A standard PID motor control loop requires $T_{e2e} < 10\,\text{ms}$. Since $2.542\,\text{ms} \ll 10\,\text{ms}$, the cloud-offloaded pipeline is acceptable **for this sensor**, but navigation decisions needing sub-5 ms response would need **edge computing** instead. > [!TIP] > **Conclusion of the derivation:** Even 200 km of fiber adds only ~1 ms of propagation delay. The dominant latency is the **serialization + scheduling** (≈ 1 ms combined). This is precisely why ROS 2 with DDS Quality-of-Service (QoS) policies can support **real-time cloud robotics**. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Communication Paradigm | Use Case | Pattern | Why | KTU Example Question | |---|---|---|---| | Camera feed (30 Hz) | Topic | High-frequency, lossy-tolerant, many subscribers | *"Publish a 30 Hz webcam stream over ROS"* | | Toggle gripper | Service | One-shot, blocking, idempotent | *"Implement a service to open/close a gripper"* | | Navigate to a room | Action | Long-running, needs progress feedback, cancellable | *"Send a goal to move_base and listen for feedback"* | | Static configuration | Parameter Server | One-time read at startup, infrequent updates | *"Read /robot_name from the parameter server"* | | Record sensor data | Bag | Offline analysis, repeatable replay | *"Record a rosbag of a SLAM session"* | ## 3.3 Exhaustive Concept Flow: First-Time `roscore` Boot Sequence 1. **User executes** `roscore` from a terminal. 2. **ROS Master starts** listening on TCP port `11311` (XML-RPC). 3. **Parameter Server starts** as an in-memory key-value dictionary. 4. **`rosout` node starts** — a special topic aggregator that prints every node's `stdout`/`stderr` to the terminal. 5. **User runs** `rosrun turtlesim turtlesim_node`. 6. The node calls `ros::init()` → connects to the Master on port `11311`. 7. Node **registers itself** with the Master, receives a unique URI (e.g., `http://robot:45463/`). 8. The Master responds with the **publisher/subscribers URI list** for any topics this node has declared interest in. 9. Node **establishes direct TCP/UDP** connection to all interested peers. 10. **No data ever flows through the Master** — only registration metadata. > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a *critical* KTU exam point. The Master is a **name service, not a router**. If the Master crashes, **existing connections survive**; only new node discovery fails. # 🟣 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid) > [!NOTE] > The following three diagrams form the **visual mnemonic backbone** of this topic. Examiners frequently award marks for correctly labeled schematics. ## 4.1 The Three-Layered ROS Architecture (Big-Picture Overview) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L1["LEVEL 1: FILESYSTEM (Disk View)"] PKG["Package: my_robot"] PKGXML["package.xml (Manifest)"] MSGF["msg/Sensor.msg"] SRVF["srv/Trigger.srv"] BLD["build/ devel/ (catkin)"] end subgraph L2["LEVEL 2: COMPUTATION GRAPH (Runtime View)"] MASTER["ROS Master + Parameter Server"] N1["Node: camera_driver"] N2["Node: image_processor"] N3["Node: cloud_slam"] T1["Topic: /camera/image_raw"] T2["Topic: /map"] S1["Service: /save_map"] A1["Action: /navigate_to_pose"] end subgraph L3["LEVEL 3: COMMUNITY (Ecosystem View)"] DIST["Distributions: Noetic / Humble"] REPO["Repositories: github.com/ros/..."] WIKI["ROS Wiki + ROS Answers"] IDX["index.ros.org (Package Index)"] end PKG --> PKGXML PKG --> MSGF PKG --> SRVF PKG --> BLD PKG -.compiles to.-> N1 PKG -.compiles to.-> N2 MASTER --- N1 MASTER --- N2 MASTER --- N3 N1 -- "publishes" --> T1 T1 -- "subscribed" --> N2 N2 -- "publishes" --> T2 T2 -- "subscribed" --> N3 N2 -- "offers" --> S1 N3 -- "calls" --> S1 N3 -- "sends goal" --> A1 L3 -.feeds.-> L1 L1 -.launches.-> L2 ``` ## 4.2 Publish–Subscribe Message Flow (The Most Important Diagram) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant P as Publisher Node
(camera_driver) participant M as ROS Master
(name service) participant S as Subscriber Node
(cloud_slam) Note over P,S: PHASE 1: REGISTRATION P->>M: registerPublisher(/camera/image_raw) M-->>P: ACK + Master URI S->>M: registerSubscriber(/camera/image_raw) M-->>S: ACK + Master URI Note over P,S: PHASE 2: PEER DISCOVERY S->>M: requestURIs(topic=/camera/image_raw) M-->>S: publisher URI = P S->>P: XML-RPC requestConnection() P-->>S: TCP connection accepted (TCPROS) Note over P,S: PHASE 3: DATA FLOW (DIRECT) loop every 33 ms P->>S: serialized sensor_msgs/Image end Note over M: Master is NOT in the data path! ``` ## 4.3 Service Call vs Action Call Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SERVICE["Service Call (Synchronous)"] C1[Client Node] -- "1. request" --> SV1[Service Server] SV1 -- "2. response" --> C1 end subgraph ACTION["Action Call (Asynchronous + Feedback)"] C2[Action Client] -- "1. goal" --> SV2[Action Server] SV2 -- "2. feedback" --> C2 SV2 -- "3. feedback" --> C2 SV2 -- "4. result" --> C2 end subgraph TOPIC["Topic (Fire-and-Forget)"] PP[Publisher] -- "continuous msgs" --> SS[Subscriber] end ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ROS 1 vs ROS 2 (Cloud Robotics Perspective) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ROS1["ROS 1 (Noetic) Stack"] R1A[Application Nodes
roscpp, rospy] R1B[TCPROS / UDPROS] R1C[rosmaster XML-RPC] R1D[Linux Kernel] end subgraph ROS2["ROS 2 (Humble / Jazzy) Stack"] R2A[Application Nodes
rclcpp, rclpy] R2B[rcl (ROS Client Library)] R2C[DDS Middleware
Cyclone DDS / Fast DDS] R2D[rmw (ROS Middleware Interface)] R2E[RTOS / Linux / Windows] end subgraph CLOUD["Cloud Robotics Bridge"] RBR[rosbridge_suite
WebSocket] MCAP[MCAP / mqtt_ros bridge] FED[Federated ROS 2 Network] end R1B -.legacy bridge.-> R2C R2C --> RBR R2C --> MCAP R2C --> FED ``` # 🔴 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Two direct questions modeled on actual KTU University Exam Part A (2 marks are for content, 1 mark for diagram/clarity). ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the Robot Operating System (ROS). With a neat sketch, explain its three-level architecture.** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** ROS is an open-source, meta-operating system that provides a structured message-passing framework for heterogeneous robot components. It is *not* a true OS but runs atop Linux/Windows.
