# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between gradient-based and metaheuristic optimisation algorithms. Give one example of each as used in cloud robotics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Gradient-Based | Metaheuristic |
|---|---|---|
| Derivative required? | **Yes** — uses $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ | **No** — uses only function evaluations |
| Convergence speed | Fast (quadratic for Newton) on smooth problems | Slower, but robust on noisy/black-box |
| Parallelism | Limited (sequential) | **Highly parallel** (population-based) |
| Example in cloud robotics | Distributed SGD for training grasp CNNs | PSO for multi-UAV trajectory planning |

**Example Algorithms:**
- Gradient-based: **SGD, Adam, L-BFGS** (used to train neural-network perception modules in the cloud).
- Metaheuristic: **Genetic Algorithm (GA), PSO, Ant Colony Optimisation (ACO)** (used for offline motion planning and hyperparameter tuning of robot policies).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of k-Means clustering in cloud robotics. Write the objective function it minimises.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role:** k-Means clustering is used in cloud robotics to *group similar robot behaviours, trajectories, or sensor patterns* without labelled data. Typical applications include:
- Segmenting robot *manipulation primitives* from demonstration data.
- Clustering *failure modes* in fleet telemetry for predictive maintenance.
- Discovering *user behaviour patterns* in service-robot logs.

**Objective Function:** k-Means minimises the *Within-Cluster Sum of Squares* (WCSS):

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i \Vert_2^2$$

where $C_i$ is the $i$-th cluster, $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$ is its centroid, and $k$ is the number of clusters. The algorithm alternates between (a) assigning points to the nearest centroid and (b) recomputing centroids, until $J$ converges.

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain in detail the Particle Swarm Optimisation (PSO) algorithm. State the velocity and position update equations. Discuss how PSO is applied to **multi-robot path planning** in a cloud environment.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Origin & Intuition:** PSO, proposed by Kennedy and Eberhart (1995), is inspired by the *collective behaviour of bird flocks and fish schools*. Each candidate solution is a "particle" that moves through the search space, influenced by its own best-known position and the swarm's best-known position.

**2. Algorithm Parameters:**
- $N$: swarm size (number of particles)
- $w$: inertia weight (typically 0.4–0.9) — controls exploration vs. exploitation
- $c_1$: cognitive coefficient (self-confidence, typically 2.0)
- $c_2$: social coefficient (swarm confidence, typically 2.0)
- $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$: random numbers for stochasticity

**3. Update Equations:**

$$\mathbf{v}_i^{t+1} = w \mathbf{v}_i^t + c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_{\text{best},i} - \mathbf{p}_i^t) + c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g}_{\text{best}} - \mathbf{p}_i^t)$$

$$\mathbf{p}_i^{t+1} = \mathbf{p}_i^t + \mathbf{v}_i^{t+1}$$

**Logic:**
- The **inertia term** $w \mathbf{v}_i^t$ keeps the particle moving in its current direction.
- The **cognitive term** $c_1 r_1(\mathbf{p}_{\text{best},i} - \mathbf{p}_i^t)$ pulls the particle towards its personal best — the *self-exploitation* component.
- The **social term** $c_2 r_2(\mathbf{g}_{\text{best}} - \mathbf{p}_i^t)$ pulls the particle towards the swarm's best — the *social-exploitation* component.

**4. Pseudocode:**

```
Initialize N particles with random positions p_i and velocities v_i
Evaluate fitness f(p_i) for each particle
Set p_best,i = p_i and g_best = argmin f(p_i)
repeat
    for each particle i do
        v_i ← w·v_i + c1·r1·(p_best,i − p_i) + c2·r2·(g_best − p_i)
        p_i ← p_i + v_i
        Evaluate f(p_i)
        if f(p_i) < f(p_best,i) then p_best,i ← p_i
    end for
    Update g_best
until stopping criterion met
```

**5. Application to Multi-Robot Path Planning in Cloud:**

In a warehouse with 50 mobile robots, each robot's path can be encoded as a sequence of waypoints. The *fitness function* combines **path length, collision-avoidance penalty, and congestion cost**. The cloud runs a parallel PSO with one particle per worker, evaluates each path through a *physics simulator* (e.g., NVIDIA Isaac Sim), and broadcasts the global best periodically. Robots receive *updated waypoints* via Wi-Fi. This achieves **global near-optimal coordination** that is impossible to compute onboard.

**6. Why Cloud?** Because evaluating 50 candidate paths × 1000 iterations × full physics = **~10⁹ simulation steps** — well beyond the capacity of an onboard CPU.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Derive the closed-form solution of linear regression using the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) method. How is this technique used in **robot sensor calibration** in a cloud robotics framework?*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Problem Setup:** Given a dataset $\{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}$, we want to find $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ minimising:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \right)^2 = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^\top (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$$

**2. Expansion:**

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{y}^\top \mathbf{y} - 2 \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$$

**3. Differentiation:**

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = -2 \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} + 2 \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta}$$

**4. Stationarity Condition:** Setting $\nabla J = 0$:

$$\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$$

**5. Closed-Form Solution:**

$$\boxed{\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}}$$

(Provided $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$ is non-singular, i.e., columns of $\mathbf{X}$ are linearly independent.)

**6. Application to Robot Sensor Calibration:**

Consider a robot manipulator whose end-effector position $p$ is related to joint angles $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ via $p = f(\boldsymbol{\theta}) + \epsilon$, where $\epsilon$ is noise. By linearising around an operating point:

$$p \approx \mathbf{J} \boldsymbol{\theta} + \boldsymbol{\beta}_0$$

The cloud aggregates $N = 10^6$ sample pairs $(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(i)}, p^{(i)})$ from all robots in the fleet and computes $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ using the closed-form OLS — a single batch matrix inversion on a GPU. The calibrated $\mathbf{J}$ is then pushed back to the fleet. This is **centralised, accurate, and amortised across the fleet** — a textbook cloud-robotics use case.

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a **cloud-based robot learning system**. Identify and explain the roles of the optimisation and data analytics modules.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Block Diagram (described textually since ASCII-friendly):**

```
[Robot Edge Layer]
   ├── Onboard perception (cameras, LiDAR)
   ├── Local control loops
   └── Telemetry uplink
            |
            v
[Secure Network] (MQTT / gRPC over TLS)
            |
            v
[Cloud Compute Layer]
   ├── Data Ingestion & Lake
   ├── Feature Engineering
   ├── Optimisation Module
   │      ├── Distributed SGD trainer
   │      ├── PSO/GA workers
   │      └── RL policy trainer
   ├── Analytics Module
   │      ├── Clustering (k-Means, DBSCAN)
   │      ├── Classification (Random Forest)
   │      └── Forecasting (LSTM)
   └── Model Registry & Deployment
            |
            v
[Operations Dashboard] → Human Operator
```

**2. Role of Optimisation Module:**
- Performs **gradient-based training** (Adam/SGD) of deep perception and policy networks using massive compute (GPU/TPU clusters).
- Runs **metaheuristic search** (PSO/GA) for hyperparameter tuning and black-box problems.
- Supports **RL training in simulation** with millions of parallel rollouts.

**3. Role of Data Analytics Module:**
- Performs **descriptive analytics** — fleet usage statistics, anomaly counts, downtime patterns.
- Performs **predictive analytics** — LSTM-based failure prediction, demand forecasting.
- Performs **prescriptive analytics** — recommends next-best-action policies via RL.

**4. Synergy:** The analytics module identifies *what to learn* (e.g., a new failure mode emerges), and the optimisation module executes the learning. Together they enable **lifelong learning at fleet scale**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Explain the **Q-Learning** algorithm. Write the Q-table update rule. Discuss how Deep Q-Networks (DQN) overcome the limitations of tabular Q-Learning in cloud robotics.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Setting:** Q-Learning is a *model-free, off-policy* reinforcement learning algorithm that learns the action-value function $Q(s, a)$ for a Markov Decision Process (MDP) with state space $\mathcal{S}$, action space $\mathcal{A}$, transition probabilities $P(s' \mid s, a)$, and reward $R(s, a, s')$.

**2. Update Rule:**

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]$$

where:
- $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ = learning rate
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ = discount factor for future rewards
- The term in brackets is the **TD-error** (Temporal-Difference error).

**3. Convergence:** Under the Robbins-Monro conditions on $\alpha$ and sufficient exploration, $Q(s, a) \to Q^*(s, a)$ with probability 1.

**4. Limitations of Tabular Q-Learning:**
- **Curse of dimensionality:** Even a modest state space (e.g., $256 \times 256$ image input) yields $65536$ states per channel — infeasible to store in a table.
- **No generalisation:** Similar states are treated independently.
- **Memory:** $10^9$ states $\times$ 4 bytes = 4 GB per action — impractical for high-dimensional robot observations.

**5. Deep Q-Networks (DQN):** Mnih et al. (2015) addressed these by using a *deep neural network* $Q(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ with parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ to approximate the Q-function. The loss is:

$$L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s') \sim \mathcal{B}} \left[ \left( r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s'; \boldsymbol{\theta}^-) - Q(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \right)^2 \right]$$

**Key innovations:**
- **Experience Replay Buffer $\mathcal{B}$:** Stores transitions; mini-batches are sampled uniformly. This *decorrelates* samples and stabilises training.
- **Target Network $\boldsymbol{\theta}^-$:** A slowly-updated copy of the online network. This *stabilises* the bootstrapping target and prevents divergence.

**6. Cloud Robotics Application:** DQN is *trained in the cloud* using millions of simulated rollouts (e.g., grasping in PyBullet). The trained $Q$-network is then deployed to the physical robot, which can pick novel objects using a single forward pass — achieving real-time inference well below 50 ms.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to define variables** — Every formula must be followed by a one-line definition of each symbol. A naked equation scores *partial* marks only.
> 2. **Confusing PSO with GA** — PSO uses *velocity* and *no crossover/mutation*; GA uses *crossover and mutation* and *no velocity*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Writing the SGD update without the learning rate $\eta$** — A frequent 1-mark loss. Always show $\eta$ explicitly.
> 4. **Skipping the convexity / invertibility assumption in OLS** — You must state *"assuming $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$ is invertible"* before writing the closed-form. This assumption is worth 1 mark.
> 5. **Describing DQN without mentioning Experience Replay and the Target Network** — These two innovations are *the* defining features of DQN vs. naive Q-learning. Omission costs 2 marks.
> 6. **Writing k-Means objective without the squared norm $\Vert \cdot \Vert^2$** — Most students forget the square; the WCSS is a *sum of squared* distances, not absolute distances.
> 7. **Not connecting to cloud** — In Cloud Robotics, *every* answer should justify **why the cloud** is needed (latency, scale, parallelism, fleet-level learning). A pure-theoretical answer without the cloud hook loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 — Optimisation & Data Analytics: 60-Second Revision**

### A. Optimisation Algorithms
- [ ] **Gradient Descent (GD):** $\mathbf{x}_{t+1} = \mathbf{x}_t - \eta \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_t)$. Use for smooth, differentiable objectives.
- [ ] **SGD:** Same form but with per-sample gradient $\nabla f_i$. Use for large datasets.
- [ ] **Momentum:** Adds $\beta \mathbf{v}_t$ for inertia. Use for faster convergence on ravine-like loss surfaces.
- [ ] **Adam:** Combines momentum + RMSProp. *Default optimizer* for deep learning in cloud robotics.
- [ ] **PSO:** Population-based, velocity + position update. Use for **multi-robot path planning**.
- [ ] **GA:** Population-based, selection + crossover + mutation. Use for **controller evolution, hyperparameter tuning**.
- [ ] **Newton's Method:** Uses Hessian $\nabla^2 f$. Quadratic convergence but $O(d^3)$ per step.

### B. Data Analytics Algorithms
- [ ] **Linear Regression (OLS):** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$. Use for **sensor calibration**.
- [ ] **k-Means:** Minimises WCSS. Use for **behaviour clustering**.
- [ ] **DBSCAN:** Density-based, finds arbitrary-shaped clusters, identifies noise. Use for **anomaly detection in fleet telemetry**.
- [ ] **Random Forest:** Ensemble of decision trees. Use for **fault classification**.
- [ ] **LSTM:** Recurrent network for time-series. Use for **predictive maintenance and demand forecasting**.
- [ ] **Q-Learning:** Tabular TD-learning. Use for **small discrete MDPs**.
- [ ] **DQN:** Deep Q-network with replay + target net. Use for **high-dimensional state spaces** (vision-based manipulation).

### C. Cloud-Specific Concepts
- [ ] **Distributed SGD:** Synchronous (AllReduce) vs. Asynchronous (Parameter Server).
- [ ] **Federated Learning:** Aggregates model updates, not raw data. Preserves privacy.
- [ ] **Cloud Offload Justification:** Latency-aware (real-time onboard, batch in cloud), bandwidth-aware (gradient compression), privacy-aware (federation).
- [ ] **MapReduce-style analytics:** Batch processing of fleet telemetry with Spark/Hadoop.

### D. Cross-Cutting Concepts
- [ ] **Convexity:** Guarantees global optimum. $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \succeq 0$ in OLS.
- [ ] **Exploration vs. Exploitation:** $\varepsilon$-greedy in RL; inertia $w$ in PSO; mutation rate in GA.
- [ ] **Convergence rate:** GD is linear with rate $\frac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1}$; Newton is quadratic.
- [ ] **Cloud-Robotics Synergy:** Optimisation provides *learning*; analytics provides *understanding*; cloud provides *scale*.

### E. KTU Exam-Day Tips
- [ ] Always state the **assumption** before a closed-form solution.
- [ ] Always define **every symbol** in every formula.
- [ ] Always connect the algorithm to a **cloud-robotics use case** for full marks.
- [ ] For 14-mark questions, target **3–4 pages** of structured answer with diagrams.
- [ ] Use **standard KTU notation**: $\mathbf{x}$ for vectors (bold), $x_i$ for components, $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ for norms, $\mathbb{R}^n$ for spaces.
- [ ] Memorise the **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet** (Section 2.4) — it covers 80% of numerical/derivation questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Mantra:** *"In cloud robotics, optimisation decides **how the robot learns**, analytics decides **what the robot learns**, and the cloud decides **how fast the fleet learns together**."* This single sentence can earn you 2–3 marks in any open-ended KTU Part-B question on this module.
